news_summary: Recent scientific breakthroughs reveal new insights into metabolism, appetite regulation during illness, and human origins, advancing our understanding of health and biology.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées scientifiques apportent un éclairage nouveau sur le métabolisme, la régulation de l'appétit en cas de maladie et les origines de l'humanité, enrichissant ainsi notre compréhension de la santé et de la biologie.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances científicos aportan nuevos conocimientos sobre el metabolismo, la regulación del apetito durante la enfermedad y los orígenes del ser humano, lo que amplía nuestra comprensión de la salud y la biología.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden system that turns brown fat into a calorie burner
articles.summary: Scientists have identified a key biological system that helps brown fat burn energy by building the networks it needs to function. A protein called SLIT3 splits into two parts, with each piece guiding the growth of blood vessels and nerves inside brown fat. These structures allow the tissue to pull in nutrients and rapidly convert them into heat instead of storing them as fat.
articles.content:
Researchers have discovered how a crucial protein switches on brown fat by helping it build the blood vessels and nerve connections needed for heat production.

The findings, published in Nature Communications, suggest a new way to tackle obesity that focuses on increasing how much energy the body burns instead of reducing appetite.

Brown Fat and How It Burns Calories

Most fat in the body is white fat, which stores excess energy and can contribute to obesity when it accumulates. In contrast, brown fat is present in smaller amounts and plays a specialized role in controlling body temperature and supporting metabolic health. When exposed to cold, brown fat uses glucose and lipids to produce heat through a process called thermogenesis.

"During thermogenesis, all of that chemical energy is dissipated as heat instead of being stored in the body as white fat," said Farnaz Shamsi, assistant professor of molecular pathobiology at NYU College of Dentistry and the study's senior author. "By rapidly taking up and using fuel sources from our bodies and the food that we eat, brown fat acts like a metabolic sink that draws in nutrients and prevents them from being stored."

Brown fat relies on dense networks of nerves and blood vessels to do its job. Nerves allow it to receive signals from the brain, which activates the tissue when the body senses cold. Blood vessels deliver oxygen and nutrients needed to generate heat and help distribute that heat throughout the body. While previous studies have mainly focused on how fat cells produce heat, less attention has been given to how these supporting networks develop and function.

SLIT3 Protein Builds Brown Fat Infrastructure

Earlier research from Shamsi's lab used single-cell RNA sequencing to identify SLIT3, a protein released by brown fat cells that may help them communicate. Once produced, SLIT3 is split into two separate pieces.

In the new study, scientists used experiments in both human and mouse cells to identify the enzyme BMP1, which cuts SLIT3 into these two fragments. Each fragment has a different role. One promotes the growth of blood vessels, while the other supports the expansion of nerve networks.

"It works as a split signal, which is an elegant evolutionary design in which two components of a single factor independently regulate distinct processes that must be tightly coordinated in space and time," noted Shamsi.

The researchers also identified a receptor called PLXNA1 that binds to one of the SLIT3 fragments and helps regulate nerve development in brown fat. In mouse studies, removing SLIT3 or the PLXNA1 receptor made the animals more sensitive to cold and less able to maintain their body temperature. Further analysis showed that their brown fat lacked proper nerve structure and an adequate network of blood vessels.

Links to Obesity and Metabolic Health

To determine whether the same mechanism exists in humans, the team analyzed fat tissue samples from more than 1,5000 individuals, including people with obesity. They focused on the gene responsible for producing SLIT3, which previous studies have linked to obesity and insulin resistance. Their results suggest that SLIT3 activity may influence fat tissue health, inflammation, and insulin sensitivity in people with obesity.

"That really got our attention, as it suggests that this pathway could be relevant in human obesity and metabolic health," said Shamsi.

A New Approach to Obesity Treatment

Most weight loss medications, including GLP-1s, work by suppressing appetite and reducing how much people eat. In contrast, targeting brown fat could increase how much energy the body uses. The new findings, including how SLIT3 splits into two parts and interacts with receptors to shape nerve and blood vessel networks, point to several potential targets for future treatments.

"Our research shows that just having brown fat isn't enough -- you need the right infrastructure within the tissue for heat production," said Shamsi.

Additional study authors include Tamires Duarte Afonso Serdan, Heidi Cervantes, Benjamin Frank, Akhil Gargey Iragavarapu, Qiyu Tian, Daniel Hope, and Halil Aydin of NYU College of Dentistry; Chan Hee Choi and Paul Cohen of Rockefeller University; Anne Hoffmann and Matthias Blüher of the University of Leipzig; Adhideb Ghosh and Christian Wolfrum of ETH Zurich; Matthew Greenblatt of Weill Cornell Medical College; and Gary Schwartz of Albert Einstein College of Medicine.

The research was supported in part by the National Institutes of Health (K01DK125608, R03DK135786, R01DK136724, RC2DK129961, R35GM150942), the G. Harold and Leila Y. Mathers Charitable Foundation, the American Heart Association (24CDA1271852), the Einstein-Mount Sinai Diabetes Center, the NYU Dentistry Department of Molecular Pathobiology, and the Boettcher Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about how brown fat burns calories through a protein system that builds necessary blood vessel and nerve networks. This finding has broad implications for obesity treatment by potentially increasing energy expenditure rather than suppressing appetite, offering a new approach to metabolic health. The study is detailed, involves human and animal data, and addresses a major public health issue.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how brown fat builds networks to burn calories
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Brown fat is a special type of body fat that helps produce heat by burning calories. Unlike white fat, which stores energy, brown fat uses energy to keep the body warm. To work properly, brown fat needs a network of blood vessels and nerves.

**What happened** – Researchers found that a protein called SLIT3 plays a key role in creating the blood vessel and nerve networks inside brown fat. SLIT3 splits into two parts: one part helps grow blood vessels, and the other helps grow nerves. This discovery was made using experiments on human and mouse cells and published in the journal Nature Communications.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows how brown fat builds the structures it needs to burn calories and generate heat. It also connects this process to obesity and metabolic health. For example, people with obesity may have differences in how this protein works, affecting their brown fat’s ability to burn energy.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to use this knowledge to develop new treatments for obesity. Instead of just reducing appetite, future medicines might help brown fat burn more calories by improving its blood vessel and nerve networks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered that the protein SLIT3 helps brown fat build the networks needed to burn calories, offering new ideas for obesity treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment la graisse brune construit des réseaux pour brûler des calories
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La graisse brune est un type spécial de graisse corporelle qui aide à produire de la chaleur en brûlant des calories. Contrairement à la graisse blanche, qui stocke l'énergie, la graisse brune utilise l'énergie pour réchauffer le corps. Pour bien fonctionner, elle a besoin d'un réseau de vaisseaux sanguins et de nerfs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert qu'une protéine appelée SLIT3 joue un rôle clé dans la création des réseaux de vaisseaux sanguins et de nerfs à l'intérieur de la graisse brune. SLIT3 se divise en deux parties : une aide à la croissance des vaisseaux sanguins, l'autre à la croissance des nerfs. Cette découverte a été faite grâce à des expériences sur des cellules humaines et de souris et publiée dans la revue Nature Communications.

**Impact** – C'est important car cela montre comment la graisse brune construit les structures nécessaires pour brûler des calories et produire de la chaleur. Cela relie aussi ce processus à l'obésité et à la santé métabolique. Par exemple, les personnes obèses pourraient avoir des différences dans le fonctionnement de cette protéine, ce qui affecte la capacité de leur graisse brune à brûler de l'énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser ces connaissances pour développer de nouveaux traitements contre l'obésité. Au lieu de réduire seulement l'appétit, les futurs médicaments pourraient aider la graisse brune à brûler plus de calories en améliorant ses réseaux de vaisseaux sanguins et de nerfs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que la protéine SLIT3 aide la graisse brune à construire les réseaux nécessaires pour brûler des calories, offrant de nouvelles pistes pour traiter l'obésité.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo la grasa marrón construye redes para quemar calorías
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La grasa marrón es un tipo especial de grasa corporal que ayuda a producir calor quemando calorías. A diferencia de la grasa blanca, que almacena energía, la grasa marrón usa energía para mantener el cuerpo caliente. Para funcionar bien, la grasa marrón necesita una red de vasos sanguíneos y nervios.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron que una proteína llamada SLIT3 juega un papel clave en la creación de las redes de vasos sanguíneos y nervios dentro de la grasa marrón. SLIT3 se divide en dos partes: una ayuda a crecer los vasos sanguíneos y la otra ayuda a crecer los nervios. Este descubrimiento se hizo con experimentos en células humanas y de ratones, y fue publicado en la revista Nature Communications.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra cómo la grasa marrón construye las estructuras que necesita para quemar calorías y generar calor. También conecta este proceso con la obesidad y la salud metabólica. Por ejemplo, las personas con obesidad pueden tener diferencias en cómo funciona esta proteína, afectando la capacidad de su grasa marrón para quemar energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar este conocimiento para desarrollar nuevos tratamientos contra la obesidad. En lugar de solo reducir el apetito, los futuros medicamentos podrían ayudar a la grasa marrón a quemar más calorías mejorando sus redes de vasos sanguíneos y nervios.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron que la proteína SLIT3 ayuda a la grasa marrón a construir las redes necesarias para quemar calorías, ofreciendo nuevas ideas para tratar la obesidad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized, glowing brown fat cell shaped like a cozy furnace at the center, surrounded by intricate branching networks of vibrant red blood vessels and delicate golden nerve fibers intertwining like a living circuit, all set against a soft, natural background of muted earth tones, symbolizing the hidden infrastructure powering heat and energy in the body.

articles.title: Scientists discover why your appetite suddenly disappears when you’re sick
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered how your body actually tells your brain to stop eating when you’re sick. In a new study, researchers found that specialized cells in the gut detect parasites and send signals that ultimately trigger the brain to suppress appetite. This process builds over time, explaining why you may feel fine at first but then suddenly lose interest in food as an infection takes hold.
articles.content:
Anyone who has had a severe stomach illness recognizes the pattern. Even after the worst symptoms fade, appetite often disappears and can take time to return. This same effect is experienced by millions of people worldwide who live with long-term parasitic worm infections. Despite how common it is, scientists have struggled to pinpoint exactly what causes this loss of appetite.

Researchers at UC San Francisco have now identified the biological pathway that links the gut's immune response to the brain during a parasitic infection. Their work shows how signals from the immune system can actively reduce the desire to eat.

"The question we wanted to answer was not just how the immune system fights parasites, but how it recruits the nervous system to change behavior," said co-senior author David Julius, PhD, professor and chair of Physiology at UCSF and recipient of the 2021 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. "It turns out there's a very elegant molecular logic to how that happens."

The study, published in Nature on March 25, uncovered an unexpected way that two types of cells communicate. This discovery may also help explain a range of digestive issues, including food intolerances and irritable bowel syndrome.

How Gut Cells Communicate With the Brain

The research focused on two uncommon cell types found in the gut. Tuft cells act as detectors that sense parasites and initiate immune defenses. Enterochromaffin (EC) cells release chemical signals that stimulate nerve pathways connected to the brain. These EC cells are known to produce sensations such as nausea, pain, and general gut discomfort, but it was unclear whether they directly interact with tuft cells.

"My lab has long been interested in how tuft cells, after they initially respond to a parasitic infection, release signals to other cell types," said co-senior author Richard Locksley, MD, a UCSF immunologist.

To investigate, first author Koki Tohara, PhD, a postdoctoral researcher at UCSF, used genetically engineered sensor cells placed next to tuft cells under a microscope. When the tuft cells were exposed to succinate, a compound released by parasitic worms, the nearby sensor cells lit up. This revealed that tuft cells were releasing acetylcholine, a signaling molecule typically associated with nerve cells.

When acetylcholine was introduced to lab-grown gut tissue containing EC cells, those cells responded by releasing serotonin. This then activated vagal nerve fibers, which carry signals from the gut to the brain.

"What we found is that tuft cells are doing something neurons do, but by a completely different mechanism," Tohara said. "They're using acetylcholine to communicate, but without any of the usual cellular machinery that neurons rely on to release it."

A Delayed Signal That Explains Appetite Loss

The researchers also found that tuft cells release acetylcholine in two separate phases. This helps explain why appetite loss often appears later rather than immediately after infection.

At first, tuft cells release a short burst of acetylcholine. As the immune response builds and tuft cells increase in number, they begin producing a slower, sustained release of the same signal. This prolonged release is strong enough to activate EC cells and send signals to the brain.

"This explains why you feel fine at first but then start to feel sick as the infection becomes established," Julius said. "The gut is essentially waiting to confirm that the threat is real and persistent before it tells the brain to change your behavior."

Broader Implications for Gut Disorders

To test whether this pathway affects behavior outside the lab, the team studied mice infected with parasitic worms. Mice with normal tuft cell function ate less as the infection progressed. In contrast, mice that lacked the ability to produce acetylcholine in their tuft cells continued eating normally. This confirmed that the signaling pathway directly drives appetite changes.

These findings could eventually help guide new treatments for symptoms linked to parasitic infections.

"Controlling the outputs of tuft cells could be a way to control some of the physiologic responses associated with these infections," Locksley said, noting that the implications may extend beyond parasites.

Tuft cells are found in several parts of the body, including the airways, gallbladder, and reproductive system, not just the gut. Disruptions in this newly identified signaling pathway may play a role in conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome, food intolerances, and chronic visceral pain.

The study was conducted in collaboration with Stuart Brierly, PhD, and his research team at the University of Adelaide in Australia.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery explaining how the gut communicates with the brain to suppress appetite during parasitic infections. This breakthrough enhances understanding of common illness symptoms and has broad implications for treating digestive disorders affecting millions worldwide, representing a meaningful advancement in health science.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover how the gut signals the brain to stop appetite when sick
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When people get sick with stomach infections or parasites, they often lose their appetite, but scientists did not fully understand how the body causes this change.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of California San Francisco (UCSF) found that special cells in the gut, called tuft cells, detect parasites and send chemical signals to other gut cells, which then send messages to the brain to reduce hunger. This process happens in two phases, explaining why appetite fades after some time rather than immediately.

**Impact** – This discovery reveals a clear biological link between the gut’s immune system and the brain’s control of appetite. It helps explain common experiences of feeling fine at first and then losing interest in food during illness. The findings also open new possibilities for treating digestive problems like irritable bowel syndrome and food intolerances by targeting this gut-to-brain communication.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to explore how controlling tuft cells’ signals could lead to new treatments for symptoms caused by parasites and other gut disorders. Future research may also investigate how these cells work in other parts of the body, such as the airways and gallbladder.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have discovered how gut cells communicate with the brain to reduce appetite during infections, offering new insights for treating digestive illnesses.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment l’intestin envoie un signal au cerveau pour couper l’appétit quand on est malade
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Quand les gens ont une infection de l’estomac ou des parasites, ils perdent souvent l’appétit, mais les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas bien comment le corps provoque ce changement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à San Francisco (UCSF) ont découvert que des cellules spéciales dans l’intestin, appelées cellules tuft, détectent les parasites et envoient des signaux chimiques à d’autres cellules de l’intestin, qui transmettent ensuite un message au cerveau pour réduire la faim. Ce processus se fait en deux phases, ce qui explique pourquoi l’appétit disparaît après un certain temps et pas immédiatement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre un lien clair entre le système immunitaire de l’intestin et le contrôle de l’appétit par le cerveau. Elle explique pourquoi on se sent souvent bien au début puis on perd l’envie de manger pendant la maladie. Ces résultats ouvrent aussi des pistes pour traiter des troubles digestifs comme le syndrome de l’intestin irritable et les intolérances alimentaires en ciblant cette communication entre l’intestin et le cerveau.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier comment contrôler les signaux des cellules tuft pour créer de nouveaux traitements contre les symptômes causés par les parasites et d’autres troubles digestifs. Ils chercheront aussi à comprendre le rôle de ces cellules dans d’autres parties du corps, comme les voies respiratoires et la vésicule biliaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert comment les cellules de l’intestin communiquent avec le cerveau pour réduire l’appétit lors d’infections, offrant de nouvelles pistes pour soigner les maladies digestives.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo el intestino envía señales al cerebro para detener el apetito cuando estás enfermo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando las personas tienen infecciones estomacales o parásitos, a menudo pierden el apetito, pero los científicos no entendían bien cómo el cuerpo provoca este cambio.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en San Francisco (UCSF) encontraron que células especiales en el intestino, llamadas células tuft, detectan parásitos y envían señales químicas a otras células del intestino, que luego envían mensajes al cerebro para reducir el hambre. Este proceso ocurre en dos fases, lo que explica por qué el apetito desaparece después de un tiempo y no de inmediato.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra una conexión clara entre el sistema inmunológico del intestino y el control del apetito en el cerebro. Ayuda a explicar por qué uno se siente bien al principio y luego pierde el interés por la comida durante la enfermedad. Los hallazgos también abren nuevas posibilidades para tratar problemas digestivos como el síndrome del intestino irritable y las intolerancias alimentarias al apuntar a esta comunicación entre intestino y cerebro.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean explorar cómo controlar las señales de las células tuft para crear nuevos tratamientos para los síntomas causados por parásitos y otros trastornos digestivos. También investigarán cómo estas células funcionan en otras partes del cuerpo, como las vías respiratorias y la vesícula biliar.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron cómo las células del intestino comunican al cerebro para reducir el apetito durante infecciones, ofreciendo nuevas ideas para tratar enfermedades digestivas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene gut-like landscape where glowing tuft-shaped cells gently release soft golden signals that flow like musical notes toward nearby enterochromaffin cells shaped like delicate lanterns emitting calming blue light, connected by fine, thread-like pathways symbolizing nerve signals traveling upward toward a peaceful, abstract brain silhouette above.

articles.title: Scientists say we’ve been looking in the wrong place for human origins
articles.summary: A fossil ape discovered in northern Egypt is reshaping the story of human evolution. The species, Masripithecus, lived about 17 to 18 million years ago and may sit very close to the ancestor of all modern apes. This finding challenges the long-standing focus on East Africa. Instead, it points to northern Africa and nearby regions as a possible birthplace of apes.
articles.content:
Researchers report that a newly uncovered fossil ape from northern Egypt is changing how scientists view early hominoid evolution. The discovery suggests that the closest ancestors of modern apes may have originated in northern Africa, rather than in East Africa, which has long been the main focus of fossil research. "[The] findings […] confirm that paleontologists might have been looking for crown-hominoid ancestors in the wrong place," write David Alba and Júlia Arias-Martorell in a related Perspective.

The fossil dates to about 17-18 million years ago and belongs to a newly identified species called Masripithecus. It is considered the closest known hominoid relative to the lineage that eventually led to all living apes, including humans. Scientists generally agree that the earliest apes (stem hominoids) first appeared in Afro-Arabia during the Oligocene Epoch more than 25 million years ago. These early apes later spread into Eurasia between about 14 and 16 million years ago during the Miocene. Still, the exact origin of modern apes, which include all living species and their last common ancestor, remains uncertain because fossils from this time are rare, scattered, and often difficult to interpret. This challenge is made worse by gaps in Africa's fossil record, where most discoveries come from a limited number of locations, leaving large areas from this period unexplored.

Masripithecus moghraensis and Early Ape Diversity

Shorouq Al-Ashqar and colleagues describe the fossil, which was found in the Wadi Moghra region of northern Egypt and dates to around 17-18 million years ago. The species, named Masripithecus moghraensis, provides new insight into ape diversity during a key period when Afro-Arabia was becoming connected to Eurasia, allowing species to spread beyond Africa.

To determine how this species fits into the evolutionary history of humans, the researchers used a Bayesian "tip-dating" method. This approach combines anatomical features with fossil ages to estimate evolutionary relationships and divergence times. Their results indicate that Masripithecus is a stem hominoid closely related to the lineage that eventually gave rise to all modern apes.

New Clues to the Origins of Modern Apes

Based on their findings, the researchers suggest that modern apes may have originated in northern Afro-Arabia, the Levant, or the eastern Mediterranean. This challenges long-standing assumptions and highlights how much remains unknown about the early evolution of apes and humans.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a new fossil ape species in northern Egypt provides significant new insights into human evolutionary origins, challenging long-held scientific assumptions and expanding our understanding of early hominoid evolution. This breakthrough has broad scientific and educational impact, contributing to knowledge about human ancestry with substantial context and detail.
articles.personality_title: New fossil in Egypt changes story of ape and human origins
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long looked to East Africa to find fossils that explain where modern apes and humans come from. But the fossil record from millions of years ago is incomplete, and many places in Africa have not been studied much.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered a fossil ape called Masripithecus moghraensis in northern Egypt’s Wadi Moghra region. This fossil is about 17 to 18 million years old. Using special dating methods, scientists found that this ape is closely related to the ancestors of all modern apes, including humans.

**Impact** – This discovery suggests that modern apes might have first appeared in northern Africa or nearby areas, not just East Africa as previously thought. It changes where scientists will look for clues about ape and human evolution and shows how much we still don’t know about our early relatives.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely explore more fossil sites in northern Africa and surrounding regions. They will use similar methods to find other fossils that can fill gaps in our understanding of ape evolution and how ancient species spread between Africa and Eurasia.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new fossil ape from northern Egypt points to northern Africa as a key place for the origin of modern apes and changes how we study human evolution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle découverte en Égypte change l'histoire des origines des singes et des humains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps cherché en Afrique de l'Est des fossiles expliquant l'origine des singes modernes et des humains. Mais les archives fossiles de cette époque sont incomplètes, et de nombreuses régions d'Afrique sont peu explorées.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert un singe fossile appelé Masripithecus moghraensis dans la région de Wadi Moghra, dans le nord de l'Égypte. Ce fossile date d'environ 17 à 18 millions d'années. Grâce à des méthodes de datation spéciales, les scientifiques ont conclu que ce singe est proche des ancêtres de tous les singes modernes, y compris les humains.

**Impact** – Cette découverte suggère que les singes modernes pourraient être apparus pour la première fois en Afrique du Nord ou dans les régions proches, et pas seulement en Afrique de l'Est comme on le pensait auparavant. Cela change les lieux où les scientifiques vont chercher des indices sur l'évolution des singes et des humains, et montre combien nous ignorons encore nos premiers ancêtres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement explorer davantage de sites fossiles en Afrique du Nord et dans les régions voisines. Ils utiliseront des méthodes similaires pour trouver d'autres fossiles afin de mieux comprendre l'évolution des singes et la manière dont ces espèces anciennes se sont répandues entre l'Afrique et l'Eurasie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau singe fossile découvert dans le nord de l'Égypte indique que l'Afrique du Nord est un lieu important pour l'origine des singes modernes et change notre façon d'étudier l'évolution humaine.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo fósil en Egipto cambia la historia del origen de los simios y humanos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han buscado durante mucho tiempo en África Oriental fósiles que expliquen de dónde vienen los simios modernos y los humanos. Pero los registros fósiles de hace millones de años son incompletos y muchas partes de África no han sido estudiadas mucho.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron un fósil de simio llamado Masripithecus moghraensis en la región de Wadi Moghra, al norte de Egipto. Este fósil tiene entre 17 y 18 millones de años. Usando métodos especiales de datación, los científicos encontraron que este simio está muy relacionado con los antepasados de todos los simios modernos, incluidos los humanos.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo sugiere que los simios modernos podrían haberse originado en el norte de África o en áreas cercanas, no solo en África Oriental como se pensaba antes. Cambia dónde los científicos buscarán pistas sobre la evolución de los simios y humanos, y muestra cuánto desconocemos sobre nuestros primeros parientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente explorarán más sitios fósiles en el norte de África y regiones cercanas. Usarán métodos similares para encontrar otros fósiles que ayuden a llenar los vacíos en nuestro conocimiento sobre la evolución de los simios y cómo estas especies antiguas se expandieron entre África y Eurasia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo fósil de simio del norte de Egipto señala que el norte de África es un lugar clave para el origen de los simios modernos y cambia cómo estudiamos la evolución humana.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned, detailed painting of an ancient northern Egyptian desert landscape at sunrise, featuring a partially unearthed, stylized fossil skull of Masripithecus moghraensis embedded in sandy rock formations near the Wadi Moghra, with subtle outlines of ape-like silhouettes blending into the distant hills, symbolizing the hidden origins and evolving lineage of modern apes emerging from this overlooked region.
